verses.dev:
ॐ पूर्णमदः पूर्णमिदं पूर्णात्पूर्णमुदच्यते ।
पूर्णस्य पूर्णमादाय पूर्णमेवावशिष्यते ॥
ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः ॥
verses.iast:
om pūrṇamadaḥ pūrṇamidaṁ pūrṇātpūrṇamudacyate |
pūrṇasya pūrṇamādāya pūrṇamevāvaśiṣyate ||
om śāntiḥ śāntiḥ śāntiḥ ||
verses.trans:
Om. That (Absolute) is whole; this (Manifest) is whole. From wholeness comes forth wholeness. Taking wholeness from wholeness, wholeness alone remains.
Om Peace, Peace, Peace.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara views this invocation as the definitive assertion of the non-dual Brahman. 'That' refers to the unmanifested, transcendent Brahman, and 'this' refers to the manifested universe. Because Brahman is indivisible, the projection of the universe does not diminish Its absolute reality. It remains completely unaffected, meaning creation is an appearance (Vivarta) rather than a physical alteration.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja interprets this as an expression of the complete fullness of God (Ishvara) in both His transcendent cause state ('that') and His effect state as the universe ('this'). The universe, consisting of sentient souls (chit) and insentient matter (achit), is fully real and forms the body of the completely perfect Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that Vishnu (the Supreme Lord) is completely full and perfect ('pūrṇam') in all times, places, and attributes. The world created by Him is also complete and fit for its purpose. No matter how many individual souls or worlds are created or sustained, the absolute independence and power of Vishnu remains totally undiminished.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo views this mantra as the core key to cosmic evolution. The divine consciousness is completely full in its transcendent status, yet it is also completely full within the physical universe. Creation is a purposeful manifestation where the Infinite involves itself in matter to progressively evolve back into explicit spiritual wholeness.

verses.dev:
ईशा वास्यमिदं सर्वं यत्किञ्च जगत्यां जगत् ।
तेन त्यक्तेन भुञ्जीथा मा गृधः कस्यस्विद्धनम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast:
īśā vāsyamidaṁ sarvaṁ yatkiñca jagatyāṁ jagat |
ten tyaktena bhuñjīthā mā gṛdhaḥ kasyasviddhanam || 1 ||
verses.trans: All this—whatsoever moves in this changing world—is enveloped by the Lord. Therefore, find your enjoyment in renunciation; do not covet what belongs to others.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara maintains that this verse is addressed strictly to the highest class of spiritual seekers—the Sannyasins (renunciates). He translates 'vāsyam' as 'to be covered or hidden'. One must cover the illusory, changing world with the realization of the absolute, unmoving Atman. Once the world is seen as identical to the Self, all desire for external objects drops away, which is true renunciation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja interprets 'īśā vāsyam' to mean that the entire universe is inhabited, ruled, and sustained by the Lord as its inner indweller (Antaryamin). Because everything belongs to God, humans have no real ownership. We should accept and enjoy whatever material goods are given to us as a divine gift, without greed or envy.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva asserts that 'Īśā' clearly establishes the absolute supremacy and independent lordship of Vishnu over the entire universe. The world is real and is owned exclusively by Him. Because the Lord rules everything, human beings must live with deep humility, joyfully accepting their dependent lot and avoiding covetousness.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo rejects the idea that this verse demands an escape from the physical world. He views it as a call to discover the divine presence within material forms. True enjoyment (bhuñjīthā) is only possible when the ego's possessive desire is replaced by an independent spiritual delight in all things as expressions of the Divine.

verses.dev:
कुर्वन्नेवेह कर्माणि जिजीविषेच्छतं समाः ।
एवं त्वयि नान्यथेतोऽस्ति न कर्म लिप्यते नरे ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast:
kurvanneveha karmāṇi jijīviṣecchataṁ samāḥ |
evaṁ tvayi nānyathesto'sti na karma lipyate nare || 2 ||
verses.trans: Doing veritably actions here, one should desire to live a hundred years. Thus it is with you, and not otherwise; action does not cling to a man who lives this way.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: To protect his non-dual view, Shankara argues that this verse does not apply to the enlightened sage of Verse 1. Instead, it is a secondary injunction for those who are physically incapable of complete renunciation. For these people, performing scriptural rituals (Karma) without personal desire is prescribed as a lesser path to slowly purify the mind.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja rejects Shankara's split and treats Verse 2 as an essential continuation of Verse 1. He states that action (Karma) is mandatory for everyone throughout their entire lives. When work is performed as an offering to the indwelling Lord, it loses its binding nature and directly aids the seeker in achieving liberation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva argues that no human being can ever completely abandon action. Performing duties according to one's innate nature and scriptural laws is a form of continuous worship of Vishnu. Because the actions are performed out of devotion to the Lord, the resulting karmic reactions do not cling to the individual soul.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees this as a pivotal verse establishing that spiritual realization must be active. Life and work are the fields for spiritual transformation. By aligning our individual will with the cosmic divine will, we can perform actions dynamically in the world without being bound by egoistic attachments.

verses.dev:
असुर्या नाम ते लोका अन्धेन तमसाऽऽवृताः ।
तांस्ते प्रेत्याभिगच्छन्ति ये के चात्महनो जनाः ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast:
asuryā nāma te lokā andhena tamasā''vṛtāḥ |
tāṁste pretyābhigacchanti ye ke cātmahano janāḥ || 3 ||
verses.trans: Sunless and demonic are those worlds, covered in blind darkness. To them go after death those people who are slayers of their own souls.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that a 'slayer of the soul' (ātmahano) is anyone who remains trapped in ignorance of their true, non-dual identity. By identifying with the transient physical body rather than the immortal Atman, they effectively choke out and deny their own true nature, casting themselves into cyclic, dark states of rebirth.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja views the 'slayer of the soul' as someone who refuses to acknowledge their true position as a loving, dependent servant of God. By acting out of pride and pursuing base material desires, they obscure their inherent spiritual consciousness and descend into lower, miserable planes of existence.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva takes a literal approach to the 'asuryā' (demonic) worlds. He states that individuals who harbor deep hatred toward the Supreme Lord Vishnu, or who spread false philosophies that deny His supremacy, are demonic souls. Upon death, they are cast into permanent dark hells (Tamas) far removed from divine light.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo interprets the 'slayer of the soul' as someone who denies either the spiritual reality or the cosmic expression of life. By locking oneself in a narrow, materialistic egoism and shutting out the divine light, a person forces their consciousness into dark, fragmented worlds of internal suffering and confusion.

verses.dev:
अनेजदेकं मनसो जवीयो नैनद्देवा आप्नुवन्पूर्वमर्षत् ।
तद्धावतोऽन्यानत्येति तिष्ठत्तस्मिन्नपो मातरिश्वा दधाति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast:
anejadekaṁ manaso javīyo nainaddeva āpnuvanpūrvamarṣat |
taddhāvato'nyānatyeti tiṣṭhattasminnapo mātariśvā dadhāti || 4 ||
verses.trans: The Self is one, unmoving, yet faster than the mind. The senses cannot overtake It, for It moved ahead of them. Remaining stationary, It outruns those who run. By Its power, the cosmic life-force sustains all activities.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara details the paradoxical nature of the Atman. It is completely unmoving ('anejat') because it is omnipresent. The mind can travel anywhere instantly in imagination, but wherever it arrives, consciousness is already there beforehand. The senses ('deva') can never grasp It because they look outward, whereas the Atman is the ultimate internal witness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja explains that the Lord is described here as possesses incomprehensible, infinite attributes. While remaining effortlessly steady in His supreme nature, His power completely pervades everything. The cosmic life-force (Matarishvan) is able to orchestrate the natural laws of the universe only because it derives its power from Him.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights the absolute supremacy of Vishnu over all cosmic entities. The lesser gods (Devas) and the wind-god (Matarishvan) cannot fully comprehend or overtake the Lord's absolute speed and power. All cosmic functions, including the distribution of life-giving waters ('apaḥ'), are performed entirely under His strict command.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo interprets this verse as an explanation of how the static Spirit supports all dynamic cosmic energy. The immutable, unmoving consciousness provides the stable base. Within this unmoving presence, the life-force (Matarishvan) can orchestrate the evolutionary play of forms and actions in the universe.

verses.dev:
तदेजति तन्नैजति तद्दूरे तद्वन्तिके ।
तदन्तरस्य सर्वस्य तदु सर्वस्यास्य बाह्यतः ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast:
tadejati tannaijati taddūre tadvantike |
tadantarasya sarvasya tadu sarvasyāsya bāhyataḥ || 5 ||
verses.trans: It moves, and It moves not. It is far, and It is near. It is within all this, and It is outside all this.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara uses this verse to demonstrate that Brahman is free from all relative attributes. To the unilluminated mind caught up in space and time, Brahman seems incredibly far away (requiring millions of lifetimes to reach). To the enlightened seer, It is nearer than anything else, acting as their own direct, inner consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja resolves this paradox through the doctrine of God's omnipresence. The Lord is infinitely far away because He resides in His supreme, transcendent abode (Vaikuntha), yet He is intimately near as the inner soul of every living being. He resides inside the atom and extends outside the cosmos.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets the verse to show the independent power of God. He moves things, but is never moved by anything else. He is far away from the wicked who lack devotion, but standing right next to the virtuous. He fills the inside of all objects and simultaneously surrounds them on the outside.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo views this verse as a critical statement of simultaneous oneness and multiplicity. It forms the basis of his philosophy: the Divine is not just a silent, motionless spirit, nor is it just a changing material world. It is both at the same time, existing concurrently as transcendent, universal, and individual reality.

verses.dev:
यस्तु सर्वाणि भूतान्यात्मन्येवानुपश्यति ।
सर्वभूतेषु चात्मानं ततो न विजुगुप्सते ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast:
yastu sarvāṇi bhūtānyātmanyevānupaśyati |
sarvabhūteṣu cātmānaṁ tato na vijugupsate || 6 ||
verses.trans: He who sees all beings in the Self, and the Self in all beings, feels no hatred or revulsion by virtue of that realization.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara asserts that when a seeker realizes the identical oneness of their own Atman with the underlying consciousness of every single creature, the psychological cause of hatred is uprooted. Hatred ('vijugupsā') requires a sense of separation from an outside entity. With non-duality realized, all basis for conflict disappears.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja explains that the devotee sees all living beings as sharing a common spiritual nature, and recognizes that every soul is sustained within the universal Self of God. Seeing the same Lord dwelling within others as He dwells within oneself replaces aversion and prejudice with universal compassion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets this from a standpoint of dualistic harmony. The realized soul sees that all living beings are entirely dependent on the Supreme Lord (Paramatman) for their existence. Because the devotee loves God above all else, seeing God's presence as the controller inside every creature inspires them to love and respect all creation.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees this as the true foundation for universal brotherhood. It is not an ethical rule, but an identity of consciousness. By expanding our individual awareness, we can experience the joys and struggles of others within our own expanded being, making hatred impossible.

verses.dev:
यस्मिन्सर्वाणि भूतान्यात्मैवाभूद्विजानतः ।
तत्र को मोहः कः शोक एकत्वमनुपश्यतः ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast:
yasminsarvāṇi bhūtānyātmaivābhūdvijānataḥ |
tatra ko mohaḥ kaḥ śoka ekatvamanupaśyataḥ || 7 ||
verses.trans: When to the knower all beings have become one with his own Self, what delusion or what sorrow can there be for him who sees this oneness?

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara emphasizes that delusion (moha) and sorrow (shoka) are the direct results of ignorance (Avidya). When absolute unity is experienced, the concept of a separate individuality vanishes. Since there is no second entity left to lose or desire, grief and psychological confusion are completely destroyed.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja interprets this 'oneness' as a unity of purpose, qualitative nature, and pervading spirit, rather than a absolute erasure of individual souls. Because the knower sees the entire universe as a unified harmonic expression of God, they accept all life events with equanimity, rising far above worldly sorrow.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva glosses 'ātmaivābhūd' to mean that all beings are completely dependent on the Supreme Atman, or that they are identical in their qualitative spiritual essence as conscious beings. The knower recognizes that Vishnu controls all outcomes perfectly, which eliminates personal anxiety and grief.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo views this verse as the realization of cosmic unity. When we see that all diverse forms are expressions of the same one Spirit, we no longer feel isolated or threatened by the world. We can meet all life experiences with an unshakeable divine delight (Ananda), which completely banishes sorrow.

verses.dev:
स पर्यगाच्छुक्रमकायमव्रणमस्नाविरं शुद्धमपापविद्धम् ।
कविर्मनीषी परिभूः स्वयम्भूर्याथातथ्यतोऽर्थान्व्यदधाच्छाश्वतीभ्यः समाभ्यः ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast:
sa paryagāccukramakāyamavraṇamasnāviraṁ śuddhampāpaviddham |
kavirmanīṣī paribhūḥ svayambhūryāthātathyato'rthānvyadadhāccāśvatībhyaḥ samābhyaḥ || 8 ||
verses.trans: He (the Self) is all-pervading, radiant, bodiless, flawless, without sinews, pure, and untouched by evil. The Seer, the Thinker, the One who is above all, Self-existent—He has duly distributed objects through eternal ages according to their nature.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that the first half of the verse uses neuter/negative adjectives to define the formless Nirguna Brahman (without a physical body or sinews). The second half shifts to masculine terms like 'Kavi' (the Seer) to show that this identical consciousness acts as the omniscient, self-existent ruler of the relative world.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja applies this entire description to the Personal God (Ishvara). God possesses a non-material, divine body that is completely untouched by material imperfections, aging, or sin. As the supreme ruler, He structures the laws of nature and karma, giving each soul exactly what it requires across lifetimes.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva emphasizes the words 'svayambhūḥ' (self-existent) and 'paribhūḥ' (above all) to contrast the absolute independence of Vishnu with the dependent nature of creation. God is pure spirit, entirely free from physical components like veins or sinews, and He eternally maintains cosmic justice and order.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo explains that this verse describes how the pure Spirit manifests its power as cosmic law. The Divine is both a silent, pure watcher and an active creator. As the Seer (Kavi) and Thinker (Manishi), the divine intelligence projects and organizes the universe, ensuring everything unfolds according to its deep, inherent truth.

verses.dev:
अन्धं तमः प्रविशन्ति येऽविद्यामुपासते ।
ततो भूय इव ते तमो य उ विद्यायां रताः ॥ ९ ॥
verses.iast:
andhaṁ tamaḥ praviśanti ye'vidyāmupāṣate |
tato bhūya iva te tamo ya u vidyāyāṁ ratāḥ || 9 ||
verses.trans: Into blinding darkness enter those who worship ignorance (ritual action alone). Into a greater darkness enter those who are devoted to knowledge (intellectual concepts alone).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara redefines these terms to avoid contradicting his doctrine. Here, 'Avidya' means standard Vedic ritual actions done for worldly gain, which leads to rebirth. 'Vidya' does not mean ultimate Self-knowledge, but rather theoretical meditation on deities. Pursuing empty theory without performing your duties fosters spiritual pride, which leads to an even 'greater darkness'.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja interprets 'Avidya' as the performance of daily duties without devotion, which leads to stagnation. However, pursuing abstract, intellectual meditation ('Vidya') while totally abandoning your duties is far worse, because it violates scriptural law and breeds an artificial sense of detachment.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that 'Avidya' means performing actions with a complete lack of spiritual understanding. 'Vidya' is interpreted as holding perverted, false knowledge about God (such as claiming the soul is identical to God). Holding a false, blasphemous philosophy lands a seeker in a much deeper spiritual darkness than simple ignorance.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo treats this as a core critique of one-sided spiritualities. 'Avidya' is the exclusive focus on the multiple, material world, which leads to materialistic ignorance. 'Vidya' is the exclusive focus on the silent, formless One. Escaping into a detached, quietist nirvana while ignoring the evolving world is an even greater error, as it rejects God's cosmic purpose.

verses.dev:
अन्यदेवाहुर्विद्ययाऽन्यदाहुरविद्यया ।
इति शुश्रुम धीराणां ये नस्तद्विचचक्षिरे ॥ १० ॥
verses.iast:
anyadevāhurvidyayā'nyadāhuravidyayā |
itya śuśruma dhīrāṇāṁ ye nastadvicacakṣire || 10 ||
verses.trans: They say that a distinct result is obtained from knowledge, and a distinct result from ignorance. Thus have we heard from the wise who explained it to us.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara states that the path of action (Avidya) leads to the world of the ancestors (Pitriloka), while the path of meditating on deities (Vidya) leads to the higher world of the gods (Devaloka). These separate results must be understood clearly so a seeker can see that neither path provides final, non-dual liberation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja explains that performance of rituals and internal meditation produce completely different, distinct benefits. Ritual action purifies the mind of old karmic blockages, while meditation develops an active love for God. Both results are necessary stages on the path to liberation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva asserts that correct knowledge of God and the clear refutation of false philosophies produce separate, vital results. One path prevents a soul from falling into worldly traps, while the other draws the soul closer to experiencing Vishnu’s grace.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo maintains that discovering the transcendent One (Vidya) and mastering the laws of material nature (Avidya) yield different powers of consciousness. A fully developed human being must master both dimensions rather than choosing one over the other.

verses.dev:
विद्यां चाविद्यां च यस्तद्वेदोभयं सह ।
अविद्याया मृत्युं तीर्त्वा विद्ययाऽमृतमश्नुते ॥ ११ ॥
verses.iast:
vidyāṁ cāvidyāṁ ca yastadvedobhayaṁ saha |
avidyayā mṛtyuṁ tīrtvā vidyayā'mṛtamaśnute || 11 ||
verses.trans: He who understands both knowledge and ignorance together, crosses over death through ignorance and attains immortality through knowledge.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that by combining ritual actions ('Avidya') with meditation on deities ('Vidya'), an individual overcomes natural promptings and death-like limitations ('crosses over death'). This combination leads them to a high heavenly realm (Brahmaloka), achieving a state of relative immortality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja sees this verse as a beautiful validation of his unified path. A seeker performs their obligatory duties without attachment ('Avidya') to destroy past sins and overcome physical obstacles ('cross over death'). Simultaneously, they practice steady devotion ('Vidya'), which grants final, absolute immortality in God's presence.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets 'Avidya' here as the active knowledge and rejection of false doctrines. By recognizing and avoiding spiritual errors, a seeker protects themselves from spiritual ruin ('crosses over death'). Then, by cultivating true, loving knowledge of Vishnu ('Vidya'), they receive salvation.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads this as a definitive statement of his evolutionary goal. We must use our knowledge of the material universe ('Avidya') to conquer physical limitations, disease, and social disorder ('crossing over death'). At the same time, we live in the consciousness of the Divine One ('Vidya') to manifest an immortal, divine life right here on earth.

verses.dev:
अन्धं तमः प्रविशन्ति येऽसम्भूतिमुपासते ।
ततो भूय इव ते तमो य उ सम्भूत्यां रताः ॥ १२ ॥
verses.iast:
andhaṁ tamaḥ praviśanti ye'sambhūtimupāṣate |
tato bhūya iva te tamo ya u sambhūtyāṁ ratāḥ || 12 ||
verses.trans: Into blinding darkness enter those who worship the unmanifested cause. Into a greater darkness enter those who are devoted to the manifested effect.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara defines 'Asambhuti' as unmanifested primordial matter (Prakriti), and 'Sambhuti' as Hiranyagarbha (the cosmic manifested soul). Worshipping unmanifested matter leads to absorption in nature, while worshipping the manifest cosmic form for supernatural powers creates an egoistic entanglement that plunges the soul into deeper ignorance.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja explains that 'Asambhuti' refers to focusing exclusively on the destruction or dissolution of the world, leading to passivity. Focusing solely on 'Sambhuti' (the creation and acquisition of temporary material objects) binds the soul to the changing world, creating an even greater distraction from God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets 'Asambhuti' as the false belief that the universe was never created and has no source. He defines 'Sambhuti' as the belief that God undergoes physical modification to become the world. Madhva argues that both views are highly flawed, but believing that God physically transforms into matter is a greater philosophical error.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo maps 'Asambhuti' to the Formless Unmanifest, and 'Sambhuti' to the cosmos of visible forms. Seeking only the unmanifest source leads to a cold, sterile asceticism. Obsessing only over temporary, external forms leads to material binding. Both extremes are incomplete paths that fragment the total truth.

verses.dev:
अन्यदेवाहुः सम्भवादन्ण्यादाहुरसम्भवात् ।
इति शुश्रुम धीराणां ये नस्तद्विचचक्षिरे ॥ १३ ॥
verses.iast:
anyadevāhuḥ sambhavādannyādāhurasambhavāt |
itya śuśruma dhīrāṇāṁ ye nastadvicacakṣire || 13 ||
verses.trans: They say that a distinct result is obtained from the manifested, and a distinct result from the unmanifested. Thus have we heard from the wise who explained it to us.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that meditating on the manifested Hiranyagarbha grants extraordinary psychic powers (Siddhis), whereas meditating on unmanifested matter (Prakriti) leads to absorption into cosmic elements. These distinct cosmic results must be recognized as temporary, finite achievements.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja states that understanding the world's temporal nature helps a seeker develop detachment from material things, while contemplating God as the transcendent source provides internal stability. Both insights serve unique, highly practical roles in a balanced spiritual life.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva argues that studying the precise creation of the universe (Sambhava) and its final dissolution (Asambhava) yield separate insights into Vishnu's incredible majesty. Understanding both processes is necessary to comprehend how completely dependent the universe is on His will.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo maintains that realizing the divine presence within the cosmic movement (Sambhuti) grants the power of divine action, while resting in the unmanifest source (Asambhuti) gives absolute peace. These separate fruits must be brought together to live a divinely integrated life.

verses.dev:
सम्भूतिं च विनाशं च यस्तद्वेदोभयं सह ।
विनाशेन मृत्युं तीर्त्वा सम्भूत्याऽमृतमश्नुते ॥ १४ ॥
verses.iast:
sambhūtiṁ ca vināśaṁ ca yastadvedobhayaṁ saha |
vināśena mṛtyuṁ tīrtvā sambhūtyā'mṛtamaśnute || 14 ||
verses.trans: He who understands both the manifested creation and the unmanifested destruction together, crosses over death through the perishable and attains immortality through the imperishable.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara replaces the word 'Sambhuti' with 'Asambhuti' in his text to maintain consistency. He explains that by meditating on the destruction of the manifest world ('vināśa'), a seeker overcomes attachment to physical death and desires. Then, by contemplating primordial matter, they achieve an exalted cosmic status.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja explains that by keeping the inevitable destruction of the physical body ('vināśa') in mind, a seeker destroys their material pride and transcends the fear of physical death. Armed with this detachment, they can worship the Lord in His grand manifestation ('sambhūti'), attaining immortal life.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva takes 'vināśa' to mean the perishable universe. By properly understanding the dependent, changing nature of matter, one avoids being trapped by worldly changes ('crosses over death'). By simultaneously knowing the eternal, creative power of God ('sambhūti'), the soul attains liberation.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo presents this as the ultimate integration of being and becoming. A seeker must navigate the physical laws of the perishable world ('vināśa') to master bodily existence and conquer material limitations. Simultaneously, they anchor their core consciousness in the supreme, manifest divine life ('sambhūti'), expressing eternal spiritual reality through a physical form.

verses.dev:
हिरण्मयेन पात्रेण सत्यस्यापिहितं मुखम् ।
तत्त्वं पूषन्नपावृणु सत्यधर्माय दृष्टये ॥ १५ ॥
verses.iast:
hiraṇmayena pātreṇa satyasyāpihiṁ mukham |
tattvaṁ pūṣannapāvṛṇu satyadharmāya dṛṣṭaye || 15 ||
verses.trans: The face of Truth is hidden by a golden vessel. O Sun, uncover it so that I, who am devoted to Truth, may behold it.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara interprets the 'golden vessel' as the intense, blinding light of the solar orb, which represents the highest peak of relative material reality. This beautiful cosmic light dazzles human eyes, preventing them from seeing the true, attribute-less Brahman that lies beyond all forms. The seeker prays for this material veil to be lifted.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja views the golden vessel as the beautiful, majestic material universe (Prakriti). The worldly mind is so infatuated by these brilliant sensory phenomena that it fails to look deeper. The devotee prays to the solar deity (as a form of God) to withdraw this outer glare so they can perceive the glorious personal form of Truth.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the 'golden vessel' is the solar disk, within which the deity Surya-Narayana resides. The outer light hides the supreme personal form of Vishnu. The genuine seeker begs the Lord to uncover His hidden face, allowing them to engage in direct, loving contemplation.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo treats the 'golden vessel' as a brilliant metaphor for our mental concepts and half-truths. Mind and material success look incredibly attractive, like gold, but they ultimately obscure the vast, supramental Truth. The evolutionary seeker demands that this intellectual veil be broken so they can perceive the direct, unmediated reality.

verses.dev:
पूषन्नेकर्षे यम सूर्य प्राजापत्य व्यूह रश्मीन्समूह तेजः ।
यत्ते रूपं कल्याणतमं तत्ते पश्यामि योऽसावसौ पुरुषः सोऽहमस्मि ॥ १६ ॥
verses.iast:
pūṣannekarṣe yama sūrya prājāpatya vyūha raśmīnsamūha tejaḥ |
yatte rūpaṁ kalyāṇatamaṁ tatte paśyāmi yo'sāvasau puruṣaḥ so'hamasmi || 16 ||
verses.trans: O Nourisher, solitary traveler, controller, Sun, child of Prajapati! Disperse Your blinding rays and gather Your intense radiance. I see Your most auspicious form. Whosoever is that Person up there—I am He!

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara highlights the phrase 'So'ham asmi' ('I am He') as the supreme realization of Advaita philosophy. As the blinding solar rays are drawn back, the artificial boundary between the observer and the observed collapses. The seeker realizes that the infinite light behind the sun is identical to the consciousness inside their own heart.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja explains 'So'ham asmi' through the lens of identity in indwelling spirit. The Person within the sun is the Supreme Lord, and because the individual soul is entirely pervaded and animated by that same Lord, the seeker can cry out 'I am He'. It is an expression of deep, intimate organic union, not an absolute erasure of individuality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva strongly objects to any non-dual interpretation of 'So'ham asmi'. He alters the reading to 'Sa aham asmi' meaning 'I am independent like Him' or translates 'Aham' based on its root meaning as 'unassailable'. He emphasizes that the soul is declaring its absolute dependence on and reflection of the supreme Purusha, not its literal equality.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees this as the realization of the Supramental Divine. By looking past the dazzling, fragmented rays of the lower mind, the seeker witnesses the unified divine consciousness. Realizing 'I am He' means acknowledging that our individual identity is an intentional expression of that same supreme, universal consciousness.

verses.dev:
वायुरनिलममृतमथेदं भस्मान्तं शरीरम् ।
ॐ क्रतो स्मर कृतं स्मर क्रतो स्मर कृतं स्मर ॥ १७ ॥
verses.iast:
vāyuranilamamṛtamathedaṁ bhasmāntaṁ śarīram |
om krato smara kṛtaṁ smara krato smara kṛtaṁ smara || 17 ||
verses.trans: May this life-breath merge into the all-pervading, immortal Prana! And may this body be reduced to ashes. O mind, remember! Remember what has been done! O mind, remember! Remember what has been done!

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara describes this as the calm composure of an enlightened person at the moment of death. The physical body is recognized as a temporary combination of elements that will inevitably return to ash. The individual breath (Prana) joins the cosmic, immortal life-force, while the true Atman remains completely untouched as the absolute, changeless witness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja explains that the devotee, facing death, consciously surrenders their life-force to God. The call to the mind/will ('Krato') to 'remember what has been done' is an appeal to the Lord to graciously remember a lifetime of loving service and devotion, ensuring a peaceful transition into His eternal spiritual abode.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva glosses 'Krato' as a direct name for Vishnu, the ultimate intelligence and creator. The verse is an urgent prayer: 'O Lord Vishnu, remember me and remember my deeds at this final hour!' The body is recognized as transient, while the individual soul is eternal and seeks shelter under God's grace.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo views this verse as a reminder of our immortal journey. The physical body may dissolve into ash, but the conscious energy and the soul's experiences are preserved. The command to 'remember' instructs the evolving soul to gather its past efforts, ensuring that its spiritual progress carries over into its next phase of manifestation.

verses.dev:
अग्ने नय सुपथा राये अस्मान् विश्वानि देव वयुनानि विद्वान् ।
युयोध्यस्मज्जुहुराणमेनो भूयिष्ठां ते नमौक्तिं विधेम ॥ १८ ॥
verses.iast:
agne naya supathā rāye asmān viśvāni deva vayunāni vidvān |
yuyodhyasmajjuhurāṇameno bhūyiṣṭhāṁ te namauktiṁ vidhema || 18 ||
verses.trans: O Fire, lead us by the good path to prosperity, O God, you who know all our deeds. Remove from us our deceitful sins. We offer you our highest salutations again and again.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that this final, humble prayer to the fire-god (Agni) applies to those seekers who followed the combined path of rituals and meditation. Because they did not achieve immediate, absolute non-dual realization during life, they pray for guidance along the 'good path' of the gods (Devayana) to achieve gradual liberation (Krama-Mukti).
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja sees this as a beautiful expression of pure devotion (Bhakti) and total self-surrender (Prapatti). The devotee asks Agni (standing in for the Supreme Lord) to burn away any lingering hidden sins or moral defects. Offering salutations ('namas') again and again signifies a complete letting go of egoistic pride.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that 'Agni' is used here as a secret name for the Supreme Lord Vishnu, meaning 'the one who leads forward'. The devotee acknowledges that God knows every secret deed and thought. They pray for protection from deceitful sins and bow down repeatedly in deep, loving submission to His independent will.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo interprets 'Agni' as the divine, flaming consciousness-force that drives all evolution. This internal flame knows the intricate layout of our lives. By surrendering to this inner force, our crooked, ignorant distortions ('eno') are purged, allowing the divine will to lead us along the true path toward spiritual fulfillment.
